title: The standard \(L\)-function for \(G_2\): a "new way"
speaker: Nadya Gurevich
abstract: We consider a Rankin-Selberg integral representation of a cuspidal (not necessarily generic) representation of the exceptional group \(G_2\). Although the integral unfolds with a non-unique model, it turns out to be Eulerian and represents the standard \(L\)-function of degree 7. We discuss a general approach to the integrals with non-unique models. The integral can be used to describe the representations of \(G_2\) for which the (twisted) \(L\)-function has a pole as functorial lifts. This is a joint work with Avner Segal.

proof_stages.label: Partial standard L-functions and expected automorphic properties
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines partial L-functions from Satake parameters and a finite-dimensional representation of the dual group, then states the expected meromorphic continuation, pole information, and functional equation for automorphic representations.

proof_stages.label: Eisenstein-series integral representation framework
proof_stages.summary: A Rankin--Selberg-type zeta integral is built by embedding G in a larger group H, restricting an Eisenstein series on H to G, and using the Eisenstein series to obtain analytic continuation of the global integral.

proof_stages.label: Unfolding to a model and the support problem
proof_stages.summary: Unfolding transforms the global integral into an integral over R(A)\G(A) involving a functional λ on π, so the method depends on whether π supports the resulting unique or nonunique model/Fourier coefficient.

proof_stages.label: Traditional unique-model Euler product and its failure for nonunique models
proof_stages.summary: The classical method uses uniqueness to factor the global integral into local zeta integrals, identifies unramified factors with the L-function, and controls ramified factors, but this route breaks down when the model is nonunique.

proof_stages.label: New-way bypass via a universal unramified identity
proof_stages.summary: The new-way method ignores the missing Eulerian factorization, proves directly that every unramified local model integral gives the desired L-factor, and then recovers the global partial L-function by enlarging finite sets of places.

proof_stages.label: Satake generating function and elimination of π and λ
proof_stages.summary: Using the Satake isomorphism, the L-factor is encoded by a generating/basic function δρ, and the desired local identity is reduced to an equality between the R-average of δρ and the explicit unfolded section f_s, independent of π and λ.

proof_stages.label: G2 polynomial correction and invertibility argument
proof_stages.summary: For the G2 standard seven-dimensional representation, δρ is unavailable explicitly, so a simpler Hecke function whose Satake transform is the L-factor times a polynomial is averaged, and the polynomial convolution is shown invertible in a completed C*-algebra to recover the desired identity.

proof_stages.label: Consequences for twisted G2 L-functions and theta lifts
proof_stages.summary: The integral is extended to twists and quasi-split D4 forms parameterized by cubic algebras, so poles of the standard L-function are read from Eisenstein residues and characterize nonvanishing of corresponding theta lifts.

examples.name: Classical symplectic doubling integral
examples.note: The case Sp_{2n}×Sp_{2n} embedded in Sp_{4n} illustrates the Eisenstein-integral framework and the resulting inner-product model, without being needed for the G2 argument.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker runs the framework on the symplectic doubling case, specifying the embedding and identifying the unfolded model as the diagonal inner product with its nonvanishing criterion.
